candidates.reasoning: The flat, arid landscape with scattered acacia-style trees and sandy, light-colored soil is highly characteristic of the Sahel region. Senegal's interior roads often feature this specific combination of paved surfaces and sandy shoulders.
The vegetation and soil composition are nearly identical to Senegal, as both share the Sahelian climate zone. The sparse, flat terrain and tree morphology are consistent with southern Mali.
The arid environment and flat topography match the landscape of Niger, though the road quality and specific tree density are slightly more common in the western Sahel.
Similar semi-arid savanna characteristics are found here, though the specific soil hue and road style are more indicative of West African Sahelian countries.

evidence: Flat terrain with light-colored sandy soil
Paved road with unpaved, sandy shoulders
High sun angle suggesting a tropical/subtropical latitude

candidates.reasoning: The presence of flat-topped Acacia species (likely Vachellia tortilis or similar) in a semi-arid Sahelian landscape is highly characteristic of northern and central Senegal. This vegetation pattern is nearly identical to neighboring Mali and Mauritania.
The sparse, drought-resistant Acacia scrubland and sandy soil are typical of the Sahelian belt in Mali. Botanically, it is indistinguishable from the similar arid zones of Senegal and Niger.

The combination of sandy substrate and scattered, umbrella-shaped Acacia trees is consistent with the southern regions of Mauritania. The vegetation density suggests a transition zone between the Sahara and the Sahel.
The arid savanna vegetation and specific Acacia morphology are common across the Nigerien landscape. It shares the same botanical profile as the other Sahelian countries mentioned.
The road quality and arid landscape are consistent with rural Senegal. It follows right-hand drive standards common in West Africa.
The infrastructure is minimal and consistent with rural Mali. It shares the same right-hand drive and road surface characteristics as Senegal.
The paved road with sandy shoulders and lack of markings is typical for rural Niger. It follows right-hand drive standards.

The image quality and the specific blur/stitching patterns are consistent with Google's coverage in Senegal. The road surface quality and the lack of shoulder markings are common in rural Senegalese regions.
The general image quality and environment match some of the limited coverage in Mali, though the road paving style is slightly more characteristic of Senegal.
The arid environment and road type are similar to Botswana, but the specific camera generation and image artifacts are more typical of West African coverage.

evidence: Google Street View camera generation/image quality
Road surface texture and paving style
Absence of road furniture (bollards/poles) in rural setting
